heroOverview: Sunburn is acute radiation-induced skin inflammation caused by overexposure to ultraviolet (UV) light, primarily UVB radiation in the 280–315 nm wavelength range. The World Health Organization estimates that 18,000–20,000 premature deaths occur globally each year from skin cancer, and five or more severe blistering sunburns before age 20 roughly double a person's lifetime risk of melanoma. Sunburn presents as erythema, pain, and heat within 3–5 hours of exposure, peaking at 12–24 hours. Most uncomplicated episodes heal without medical treatment in three to seven days, but the cumulative DNA damage from repeated sunburns is irreversible and underlies the majority of keratinocyte skin cancers.

keyStats.prevalence: About 33% of adults and 69% of adolescents in the United States report at least one sunburn per year (CDC, 2023)
keyStats.demographics: Most frequent in fair-skinned individuals (Fitzpatrick types I–III), children, and outdoor workers; women report slightly higher rates in surveys
keyStats.avgAge: Highest incidence in adolescents aged 11–18 and young adults aged 18–29
keyStats.globalCases: UV radiation is the leading environmental cause of skin cancer worldwide; WHO classifies tanning beds as a Group 1 carcinogen

definition: Sunburn (ICD-10: L55.9) is a self-limiting acute inflammatory reaction of the skin produced by excessive ultraviolet radiation exposure, predominantly UVB. The photons are absorbed by DNA bases forming cyclobutane pyrimidine dimers that trigger keratinocyte apoptosis, release of prostaglandins E2 and I2, and recruitment of inflammatory mediators, producing the characteristic erythema, oedema, and pain. Severity is graded by the depth of injury: first-degree sunburn involves only the epidermis; second-degree sunburn extends into the dermis and may produce blisters. ICD-10 L55.9 (sunburn, unspecified) is used when depth is not formally documented. Sunburn is considered a Dermatology condition affecting the Skin & Dermatology body system. Beyond the acute injury, each UV exposure event contributes to cumulative photoageing and elevates the lifetime risk of squamous cell carcinoma, basal cell carcinoma, and melanoma through persistent somatic mutations in tumour-suppressor genes such as TP53.

typesClassification.description: Confined to the epidermis; presents as uniform red, tender, dry skin that blanches under pressure. Resolves in three to five days with peeling but no scarring.
Extends into the upper dermis; characterised by blistering, intense pain, oedema, and weeping. Risk of secondary infection. Coded separately when the clinical picture is clear.
Used when depth is not clinically assessed or documented; the most commonly assigned code in outpatient and emergency settings.
Cumulative UV exposure over years produces premature skin ageing, solar lentigines, actinic keratoses, and ultimately skin cancer, representing the long-term consequence of repeated sunburn episodes.

primarySymptoms: Erythema — diffuse redness of sun-exposed skin appearing 3–5 hours after UV exposure, confirming the inflammatory response has started.
Skin pain and tenderness — burning or stinging sensation, often described as feeling like heat radiating from the skin surface, which worsens with contact or friction.
Warmth and heat — the affected area feels noticeably hotter than adjacent unexposed skin due to increased dermal blood flow.
Swelling or oedema — mild to moderate puffiness over sun-exposed areas, most visible on the face, lips, and tops of the feet.
Blistering — fluid-filled vesicles or bullae indicating second-degree depth; these are fragile and prone to rupture, raising infection risk.
Skin peeling — desquamation of the damaged epidermis beginning two to four days after exposure as dead keratinocytes are shed.
Itching (pruritus) — common during the healing and peeling phase as new skin forms beneath the damaged layer.
Headache and fatigue — systemic symptoms reflecting UV-related oxidative stress and, in more severe cases, accompanying heat exposure.
Fever and chills — in severe or extensive sunburn (greater than 15% body surface area) systemic inflammatory response may produce low-grade fever.
Nausea — in widespread or prolonged UV exposure, particularly when combined with heat exhaustion, gastrointestinal symptoms may accompany the skin reaction.
earlyWarningSigns: Skin that feels tighter or slightly warmer than usual within 1–2 hours of sun exposure.
A faint pink flush that is more noticeable when returning from outdoors.
Mild stinging when cold water contacts the skin shortly after UV exposure.
Slight increase in skin sensitivity or tenderness to touch in exposed areas.
emergencySigns: Extensive blistering covering more than 10–15% of the body surface, particularly in children.
High fever above 39°C, severe headache, confusion, or rapid pulse that may indicate heat stroke alongside severe sunburn.
Signs of dehydration such as dry mouth, dizziness, and very reduced urine output.
Severe pain, rapidly spreading redness, warmth, or red streaks suggesting secondary skin infection or cellulitis.
Eye pain, blurred vision, or sensitivity to light following UV exposure, suggesting photokeratitis.

causes.description: UVB (280–315 nm) is the primary cause of sunburn; it is absorbed directly by DNA, forming pyrimidine dimers that trigger keratinocyte apoptosis and prostaglandin-mediated vasodilation. Peak UVB intensity occurs between 10 a.m. and 4 p.m. at solar noon.

causes.cause: UVA radiation contributing to deep dermal damage
causes.description: UVA (315–400 nm) penetrates more deeply than UVB, generates reactive oxygen species that damage cellular membranes and connective tissue, and is transmitted through glass; it amplifies burning in high-altitude or reflection settings.

causes.cause: Artificial UV sources (tanning beds and sunlamps)
causes.description: Commercial tanning beds emit predominantly UVA at intensities up to 10–15 times that of midday sun, while some also emit significant UVB; the WHO classifies them as Group 1 human carcinogens. Each tanning bed session before age 35 raises melanoma risk by approximately 59–75%.

Snow reflects up to 80% of UV, water up to 25%, and sand up to 15% of incident UV; people at beaches, ski slopes, or near water can receive substantial indirect exposure even in shade, creating an underestimation of exposure dose.

UV intensity increases by approximately 4% per 300 metres of altitude and is highest near the equator; travellers to mountains or tropical destinations receive UV doses substantially above their usual exposure, shortening the minimum erythema dose.

Numerous drugs lower the UV dose required to produce sunburn: tetracycline antibiotics, fluoroquinolones, thiazide diuretics, NSAIDs, retinoids, and certain antifungals. The mechanism involves drug photoproducts generating free radicals on skin.

riskFactors.description: People with light skin, blonde or red hair, and blue eyes have less melanin shielding their DNA; they burn after as little as 10–15 minutes of midday summer sun and have substantially higher rates of all skin cancers.

riskFactors.factor: Duration and intensity of UV exposure without protection
riskFactors.description: Longer exposure periods, midday sun, and high UV index days (7 or above) dramatically raise cumulative UV dose and sunburn probability; avoiding peak-hour exposure is the single most effective protective behaviour.

riskFactors.factor: Failure to use sun protection factor (SPF) sunscreen
riskFactors.description: SPF 30+ broad-spectrum sunscreen blocks approximately 97% of UVB; not applying it, using too little (less than 2 mg/cm²), or failing to reapply every two hours substantially negates protection.
Living or travelling near the equator or at altitude increases ambient UV irradiance; Australian and equatorial populations face several-fold higher UV exposure than northern Europeans at sea level.
Skin in childhood has thinner stratum corneum and lower melanin density; children burn faster and with lower UV doses. Most lifetime UV exposure and skin cancer risk accumulates before age 18.

Patients taking tetracyclines, quinolones, hydrochlorothiazide, or retinoids have a markedly lower threshold for UV-induced erythema; sun avoidance or physical sun protection should accompany these medications.

Each blistering sunburn episode further depletes the skin's repair capacity and accumulates p53 mutations; five or more blistering burns before age 20 approximately doubles the lifetime risk of melanoma.

Transplant recipients and others on long-term immunosuppression have up to 100 times the general population risk of squamous cell carcinoma, and UV-induced DNA damage escapes normal surveillance more readily.

affectedDemographics: Fair-skinned individuals of Northern European, Celtic, or Scandinavian ancestry
Children and teenagers, who accumulate most lifetime UV exposure
Outdoor workers including construction workers, farmers, and lifeguards
Beachgoers, skiers, and recreational water-sport enthusiasts
People in equatorial and high-altitude regions
Tanning bed users, disproportionately young women
diagnosisOverview: Sunburn is a clinical diagnosis based on the history of UV exposure and physical examination of the skin. No laboratory or imaging tests are needed in typical presentations. The clinician assesses sun exposure duration, time of day, geographic location, use of sunscreen and protective clothing, and whether photosensitising drugs are involved. The skin is examined for the extent of erythema (using the rule of nines to estimate percentage of body surface area affected), depth of injury (presence of blisters distinguishes second-degree from first-degree burn), and signs of secondary infection. In patients with extensive burns, fever, or systemic symptoms, basic blood tests to assess fluid and electrolyte status may be warranted. Differentiation from drug-induced phototoxicity requires a medication review; from allergic photodermatitis when the rash is vesicular or eczematous beyond sun-exposed margins; from lupus when a butterfly rash on the face is prominent or systemic features are present. Photokeratitis should be separately evaluated when eye symptoms are reported.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms the diagnosis and assesses severity by mapping erythema distribution, checking for blistering, and quantifying body surface area involved.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician will ask when and where you were exposed to the sun, whether you used sunscreen, and will examine the affected skin under good lighting; no instruments are required for most cases.

diagnosticTests.test: Body surface area assessment (rule of nines)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Estimates the extent of skin involvement, which guides decisions about hospitalisation, fluid replacement, and wound care.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician uses body region percentages (each arm 9%, each leg 18%, trunk 36%, head 9%) to calculate total involved area; this takes under five minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies whether a drug is lowering the sunburn threshold, which changes management and requires counselling about future sun protection.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You will be asked about all current prescriptions, over-the-counter medicines, and supplements; a comprehensive list helps the clinician identify photosensitising agents.

diagnosticTests.test: Fluid and electrolyte blood tests (in severe cases)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses for dehydration and electrolyte imbalance in extensive burns, particularly in young children or elderly patients.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw from the arm; results are available within a few hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies bacterial organisms in ruptured blisters showing signs of infection such as increasing pain, pus, or surrounding cellulitis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A sterile swab is taken from the wound surface; results take 24–48 hours.

treatmentOverview: The management of sunburn focuses on reducing inflammation, relieving pain, maintaining skin hydration, and preventing secondary infection. There is no treatment that reverses the UV-induced DNA damage; all interventions are symptomatic. Immediate first aid involves removing the patient from UV exposure and cooling the skin with cool (not iced) water baths or compresses applied for 10–15 minutes at a time. Oral non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen or aspirin reduce prostaglandin-mediated inflammation and pain when started early; they are most effective within the first 24 hours. Topical emollients, aloe vera preparations, and moisturisers restore the skin barrier and alleviate tightness and peeling. Intact blisters should not be deliberately ruptured; if they break, the area is cleansed gently and covered with a non-adherent dressing. Short courses of mild-to-moderate topical corticosteroids are sometimes used in severe first-degree burns on small areas, although evidence for their systemic benefits in sunburn is limited. In extensive second-degree burns, or when systemic illness accompanies the skin injury, clinical management resembles that of thermal burns: fluid resuscitation, infection monitoring, and wound care under specialist supervision. Fever, headache, and systemic symptoms resolve with appropriate analgesia and hydration in the majority of patients within 48–72 hours.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral NSAIDs (ibuprofen, aspirin, naproxen)
medicalTreatments.description: Systemic NSAIDs inhibit cyclooxygenase enzymes, reducing prostaglandin E2 and I2 production, which are the primary mediators of sunburn erythema and pain. Most effective when started within 6–8 hours of UV exposure and continued for 48 hours.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Studies show NSAIDs reduce the erythema index by approximately 20–30% and significantly decrease pain scores compared with placebo when started early.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical emollients and moisturisers
medicalTreatments.description: Apply generously to the affected skin to restore the epidermal barrier, reduce transepidermal water loss, and relieve tightness and itching. Fragrance-free preparations containing ceramides, hyaluronic acid, or shea butter are preferred.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Consistently recommended in clinical guidelines; patient-reported pain and dryness improve by roughly 40–50% compared with no treatment in controlled studies.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical aloe vera
medicalTreatments.description: Aloe vera gel contains glycoproteins that reduce inflammation and polysaccharides that support skin repair; it has a well-established safety profile and provides cooling relief on application.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Meta-analyses suggest topical aloe vera shortens healing time of first-degree burns by approximately 8–9 days compared with conventional dressings in some comparative studies.

medicalTreatments.name: Cool water compresses and baths
medicalTreatments.description: Cooling reduces the temperature of inflamed skin, constricts blood vessels, and provides immediate pain relief; cool (not cold) water at 15–25°C is used to avoid cold injury or systemic hypothermia in children.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Widely recommended as first-line immediate care; reduces self-reported pain intensity within 10–15 minutes in virtually all patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical low-potency corticosteroids (hydrocortisone 1%)
medicalTreatments.description: Applied to limited areas of severe erythema to suppress inflammation; not used on ruptured or infected skin. Evidence for benefit beyond emollients and NSAIDs in sunburn is modest, so use is reserved for significant localised inflammation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical trials in sunburn show modest reduction in erythema (approximately 15–20%) compared with vehicle alone; benefit is most apparent in the first 24–48 hours.

medicalTreatments.name: Antibiotic treatment for secondary infection
medicalTreatments.description: Ruptured blisters or areas of cellulitis require topical antiseptics (chlorhexidine or mupirocin) for localised infection or systemic oral antibiotics (e.g., flucloxacillin, cephalexin) for spreading infection, guided by culture results.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Standard antibiotics achieve clinical cure in greater than 90% of uncomplicated skin infections when the organism is susceptible.

alternativeTreatments.name: Calendula-based topical preparations
alternativeTreatments.description: Calendula (marigold) extracts have anti-inflammatory and wound-healing properties; they are used in natural skincare products for sunburn relief, though randomised trial evidence specific to UV burns is limited.

alternativeTreatments.name: Chamomile compresses
alternativeTreatments.description: Chamomile contains the flavonoid apigenin, which has anti-inflammatory effects; cool chamomile tea compresses are a traditional remedy for mild erythema, with a good safety profile.

alternativeTreatments.name: Oral antioxidant supplementation (vitamins C and E)
alternativeTreatments.description: Some evidence suggests that regular combined supplementation with vitamins C and E modestly raises the minimal erythema dose, offering marginal photoprotection as a preventive adjunct rather than a treatment for active sunburn.

whySeeSpecialist: Most mild sunburns can be managed at home. A dermatologist should be consulted when blistering affects large body surface areas, when skin fails to heal within two weeks, when recurrent sun sensitivity appears even with minimal exposure (suggesting a photosensitivity disorder), or when lesions persist after healing that may represent actinic keratosis or early skin cancer. Sun-damaged skin with multiple actinic keratoses warrants annual dermatological surveillance.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a board-certified dermatologist with experience in photobiology and skin cancer surveillance. For children with extensive sunburn or those on photosensitising medications, a dermatologist with paediatric experience is preferable. Ask whether the practice offers dermoscopy, photopatch testing, and regular mole mapping if you have a personal or family history of skin cancer.
preventionStrategies: Apply SPF 30+ broad-spectrum sunscreen to all exposed skin 20–30 minutes before going outdoors, and reapply every two hours and after swimming or sweating.
Seek shade between 10 a.m. and 4 p.m. when UV index is at its peak, particularly at high altitudes or tropical latitudes.
Wear protective clothing: tightly woven long-sleeved shirts, wide-brimmed hats (minimum 7.5 cm brim), and UV-blocking sunglasses.
Check the daily UV index forecast and restrict unprotected outdoor activity when it exceeds 6.
Avoid all forms of artificial UV tanning (tanning beds and sunlamps), which provide no health benefit and increase skin cancer risk.
Review current medications with a clinician for photosensitising properties before sun-intensive activities.
lifestyleModifications: Make sunscreen application a daily habit, not just on beach days; most UV exposure accumulates from incidental daily activities.
Rehydrate well after sun exposure with water and electrolytes to compensate for increased insensible fluid losses.
After a blistering sunburn, inspect the healed skin for new pigmented lesions or persistent rough patches over the following months.
Educate children early about sun protection habits; most lifetime skin cancer risk derives from UV exposure in childhood.
Use UV-blocking window film in cars and homes if you spend prolonged time near windows.
Limit sun exposure during the healing phase as recently burned skin is hypersensitive to further UV damage.

recommended: Generous fluid intake — water and electrolyte beverages — to support systemic hydration while the skin barrier is impaired
Foods rich in antioxidants (vitamins C and E, carotenoids) such as citrus fruits, leafy greens, and tomatoes to support skin repair
Protein-adequate diet to provide amino acids for epidermal regeneration
Omega-3 fatty acids from oily fish, which have modest anti-inflammatory properties
avoid: Alcohol, which causes vasodilation and worsens dehydration
Very salty or processed foods that increase fluid requirements
Caffeine in excess when systemic heat exposure has accompanied sunburn, as it adds to dehydration

exerciseGuidelines: Avoid outdoor exercise in direct sunlight while sunburned skin is inflamed; physical activity increases body temperature, worsens dehydration, and may aggravate erythema through friction. Indoor or shaded activity is acceptable provided the patient is well hydrated. Resume normal outdoor exercise once the erythema and pain have fully resolved, with thorough sun protection in place.
dailyManagement: Apply an emollient or aloe vera gel two to three times daily to the affected area until peeling is complete.
Take ibuprofen or aspirin at regular intervals for the first 24–48 hours to reduce inflammation.
Keep blisters intact; if they break, clean gently with mild soap and water and cover with a non-stick dressing.
Wear loose, soft fabrics (cotton) over burned areas to minimise friction and irritation.
Stay out of the sun entirely until the skin has fully healed, then resume with full protective measures.
prognosis: Uncomplicated first-degree sunburn resolves completely within three to five days with good symptomatic care and no lasting structural damage beyond the episode. Second-degree blistering sunburn heals in one to three weeks; superficial second-degree burns leave minimal residual change, while deeper burns may cause temporary post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation or, rarely, scarring. The acute episode carries no long-term functional impairment. The clinical concern is the cumulative oncological risk: each episode of blistering sunburn permanently increases somatic mutation burden in the skin, and the WHO estimates that avoiding excess UV exposure could prevent 90% of skin cancers. People with a history of extensive sunburn require long-term dermatological monitoring for actinic keratoses and skin cancer.
recoveryTimeline: Erythema and peak pain occur at 12–24 hours after UV exposure and begin to fade by 48–72 hours. Peeling of the damaged epidermis starts at two to four days and completes by seven to ten days in typical first-degree sunburn. Second-degree burns with blistering take one to three weeks to re-epithelialise. Systemic symptoms such as fever and headache, when present, resolve within 24–48 hours with appropriate fluids and analgesics.
complications: Skin infection — ruptured blisters can become colonised by Staphylococcus aureus or Streptococcus pyogenes, producing impetigo or cellulitis that requires antibiotic treatment.
Heat exhaustion and heat stroke — severe sunburn in high-temperature environments frequently coexists with heat illness, which can be life-threatening and requires emergency cooling.
Actinic keratosis — cumulative UV damage from repeated sunburns produces rough, scaly precancerous lesions on sun-exposed skin that require monitoring and treatment.
Melanoma and non-melanoma skin cancer — multiple blistering sunburns are an established independent risk factor for melanoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and basal cell carcinoma over the long term.
Photoageing — irreversible structural damage to dermal collagen and elastin from cumulative UV exposure produces premature wrinkles, solar lentigines, and uneven pigmentation.
Photokeratitis — UV radiation that reaches the cornea produces painful eye inflammation (snowblindness or welder's flash equivalent), with recovery taking 24–72 hours.

supportResources.name: WHO — Ultraviolet Radiation and Health
supportResources.description: WHO fact sheet on UV radiation, its health effects, and global prevention recommendations.

supportResources.name: CDC — Sun Safety
supportResources.description: CDC guidance on protecting skin from UV radiation, including sunscreen use, protective clothing, and UV index monitoring.

supportResources.name: AAD — Sunburn
supportResources.description: American Academy of Dermatology patient page on treating sunburn and reducing skin cancer risk.

sources.title: WHO — Ultraviolet radiation (UV): Global solar UV index — a practical guide
Narayanan DL, Saladi RN, Fox JL. Ultraviolet radiation and skin cancer. International Journal of Dermatology, 2010
CDC — Skin Cancer Prevention Progress Report 2023
Lim HW et al. The risks of indoor tanning: AMA Council on Science and Public Health. JAMA Dermatology, 2015
IARC Working Group. Radiation: A Review of Human Carcinogens — Use of UV-emitting tanning devices. IARC Monographs Vol 100D, 2012
Sambandan DR, Ratner D. Sunscreens: An overview and update. Journal of the American Academy of Dermatology, 2011

confusedWithConditions.name: Allergic contact dermatitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Allergic contact dermatitis is triggered by a skin allergen, not UV, and appears beyond sun-exposed areas; patch testing distinguishes it from photosensitivity reactions.

confusedWithConditions.name: Photocontact dermatitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Photocontact dermatitis requires both a photosensitising chemical on the skin and UV activation; the distribution mirrors chemical contact areas rather than general sun exposure.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lupus erythematosus
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lupus can produce a photosensitive butterfly rash on the face, but it is accompanied by systemic features (joint pain, fatigue, ANA positivity) and persists without ongoing UV exposure.

confusedWithConditions.name: Rosacea
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Rosacea produces chronic facial erythema and flushing that is not directly proportional to UV exposure; it follows a relapsing-remitting course without blistering or peeling.

relatedConditions.name: Melanoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Repeated blistering sunburns are among the strongest modifiable risk factors for melanoma, the deadliest form of skin cancer.

relatedConditions.name: Actinic keratosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Actinic keratoses are UV-induced precancerous lesions that develop on chronically sun-damaged skin; a history of sunburns directly raises their incidence.

relatedConditions.name: Eczema
relatedConditions.relevance: Impaired skin barrier in eczema lowers UV tolerance; eczema patients may burn more easily and face additional challenges in sun protection.

relatedConditions.name: Vitiligo
relatedConditions.relevance: Depigmented patches in vitiligo lack melanin protection and are far more susceptible to sunburn, requiring targeted high-SPF protection.

faqs.question: How long does sunburn take to appear?
faqs.answer: Redness from sunburn usually begins 3–5 hours after UV exposure and reaches its peak intensity at 12–24 hours. The skin may not look visibly burned immediately after coming inside, which leads many people to underestimate how much UV they received.

faqs.question: What is the best immediate treatment for sunburn?
faqs.answer: Get out of the sun, cool the skin with cool (not cold or iced) water baths or compresses for 10–15 minutes, take ibuprofen or aspirin for pain and inflammation, and apply an unscented moisturiser or aloe vera gel. Avoid tight or rough clothing over the burned area.

faqs.question: Should I pop sunburn blisters?
faqs.answer: No. Intact blisters act as a natural wound covering that protects against infection. If a blister breaks on its own, clean the area gently with mild soap and water and cover with a non-stick bandage. Deliberately bursting blisters raises the risk of skin infection.

faqs.question: How can I relieve sunburn pain quickly?
faqs.answer: Oral ibuprofen or aspirin are the most effective options for reducing both pain and redness when started early. Cool compresses and aloe vera gel provide rapid topical relief. Avoid butter, oil, or petroleum-based products on freshly burned skin, as they trap heat.

faqs.question: When should I see a doctor for sunburn?
faqs.answer: Seek medical attention if blistering covers a large area, especially in a child; if you have a high fever, chills, severe headache, or confusion; or if signs of infection appear in ruptured blisters such as spreading redness, pus, or increasing pain.

faqs.question: Does sunburn turn into a tan?
faqs.answer: In some people, after the red phase fades and the skin peels, the skin may appear slightly darker as melanin production increases as a repair response. However, this tan does not undo the DNA damage caused by the burn, and it provides very little protective benefit against further UV.

faqs.question: Can you get sunburned on a cloudy day?
faqs.answer: Yes. Clouds block some visible light but transmit up to 80% of UV radiation. People frequently underestimate UV exposure on overcast days and skip sunscreen, which accounts for many sunburns at higher latitudes.

faqs.question: How do I know if I have sun poisoning?
faqs.answer: Sun poisoning refers to a severe systemic reaction to intense UV and heat exposure, including symptoms beyond the skin: nausea, vomiting, dizziness, headache, fever, and dehydration. It requires medical assessment, oral rehydration or IV fluids if severe, and rest in a cool environment.

faqs.question: How does sunscreen SPF work?
faqs.answer: SPF measures how much longer it takes protected skin to redden compared with unprotected skin. SPF 30 blocks about 97% of UVB and SPF 50 blocks about 98%. No sunscreen blocks 100% of UV. Broad-spectrum sunscreens also protect against UVA. Applying too little or not reapplying reduces actual protection significantly.

faqs.question: Can sunburn cause long-term skin damage?
faqs.answer: Yes. UV-induced DNA mutations accumulate with each sunburn episode. Multiple blistering burns, particularly before age 20, roughly double the lifetime risk of melanoma. Cumulative UV exposure also causes premature ageing, actinic keratoses, and the majority of basal cell and squamous cell carcinomas.

faqs.question: How many SPF should I use?
faqs.answer: Dermatologists recommend at least SPF 30 broad-spectrum sunscreen for daily use and SPF 50 for extended outdoor activities. Apply 2 mg per square centimetre of skin — about a shot glass worth for the body — and reapply every two hours and after water or sweat.

faqs.question: Is aloe vera actually good for sunburn?
faqs.answer: Aloe vera gel has genuine anti-inflammatory and moisturising properties that provide cooling relief and support skin healing. It is most effective for first-degree burns. Choose a product that is at least 70% aloe vera without added fragrances or alcohol, which can irritate burned skin.

faqs.question: Can babies get sunburned?
faqs.answer: Yes, and infants are especially vulnerable because their skin has a thinner stratum corneum and lower melanin density. Babies under 6 months should be kept out of direct sun entirely. For older infants, use SPF 30+ sunscreen sparingly on exposed skin, shade, and protective clothing.

faqs.question: Does sunburn increase the risk of skin cancer?
faqs.answer: Yes. Each episode of sunburn, particularly blistering sunburn, increases somatic UV-induced mutations in skin cells including p53 tumour-suppressor gene damage. Five or more blistering sunburns before age 20 approximately doubles lifetime melanoma risk. Most non-melanoma skin cancers are also directly attributable to cumulative UV exposure.

faqs.question: Why does skin peel after sunburn?
faqs.answer: Peeling is the body shedding dead and damaged keratinocytes that underwent apoptosis in response to UV-induced DNA damage. It typically begins 2–4 days after exposure and continues for several days. Moisturising the skin reduces discomfort, but pulling or peeling skin early can damage the healing layer beneath.

faqs.question: What is the UV index and how should I use it?
faqs.answer: The UV index is an international scale from 0 to 11+ indicating the intensity of UV radiation at ground level on a given day. Index 3–5 is moderate and warrants sunscreen; 6–7 is high; 8 and above is very high to extreme and requires maximum protection. Check the UV index in your weather app before outdoor activities.

faqs.question: Can you get sunburned through a car window?
faqs.answer: Standard car windows block most UVB but transmit up to 75% of UVA radiation. This is why drivers often develop asymmetric photoageing on the left arm and side of the face in countries with left-hand traffic. UV-blocking window film or an SPF 30 sunscreen applied before driving offers protection.

faqs.question: Does vinegar or milk help sunburn?
faqs.answer: Cool milk compresses are sometimes used as a home remedy; the protein and fat content may provide mild soothing relief by cooling the skin, but there is no clinical evidence they accelerate healing. Vinegar is acidic and may irritate inflamed skin. Cool water, aloe vera, and emollients are better-evidenced options.

faqs.question: How can I prevent sunburn while still enjoying the outdoors?
faqs.answer: Seek shade during peak UV hours (10 a.m. to 4 p.m.), wear wide-brimmed hats and UV-protective clothing, apply SPF 30+ sunscreen generously and reapply every two hours, and use UV-blocking sunglasses. Check the daily UV index and plan activities before or after peak hours when index is high.

metaDescription: Sunburn is UV-induced skin inflammation causing redness, pain and blisters. Learn first aid, treatment options and how to prevent lasting skin damage.
